evidence: High-ceremony, restating comments are pervasive in domain structs/enums where names are already self-explanatory (for example nearly every field in `crates/temper-evolution/src/records.rs` and `crates/temper-optimize/src/types.rs`).

Schema helpers in `crates/temper-observe/src/schema.rs` use extensive table-style and per-item narration for straightforward constant arrays and trivial membership checks, increasing maintenance surface without adding much intent.

`crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs` re-exports `PostgresRecordStore` while also suppressing deprecation warnings to keep exporting deprecated `RecordStore` (`#[allow(deprecated)] pub use store::RecordStore;`), indicating old/new API coexistence.
`crates/temper-cli/src/util.rs` and `crates/temper-mcp/src/repl.rs` are thin re-export shims that preserve old access paths without explicit migration deadlines or removal plan.

`ToolRegistry::execute` returns `Result<ToolResult>` while `ToolResult` itself has `Error(String)`, creating two overlapping failure channels (`crates/temper-agent-runtime/src/tools/mod.rs:27`, `crates/temper-agent-runtime/src/tools/mod.rs:55`).

`ToolRegistry::to_cedar` takes untyped `serde_json::Value` input but is infallible (`-> CedarMapping`), unlike adjacent APIs that surface typed errors (`crates/temper-agent-runtime/src/tools/mod.rs:58`, `crates/temper-mcp/src/error.rs:16`).

Public OData parsing API is duplicated at two import depths (`temper_odata::parse_filter` and `temper_odata::query::parse_filter`), encouraging inconsistent call sites (`crates/temper-odata/src/lib.rs:19`, `crates/temper-odata/src/query/mod.rs:9`).

Crate-level error contracts are inconsistent and mostly stringly-typed across sibling APIs (`AuthzError`, `ObserveError`, `McpError`), reducing predictable cross-crate handling (`crates/temper-authz/src/error.rs:5`, `crates/temper-observe/src/error.rs:7`, `crates/temper-mcp/src/error.rs:16`).

Deprecated API is still re-exported at crate root (`pub use store::RecordStore`), so consumers see both modern and legacy store surfaces (`crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs:21`, `crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs:28`).

findings.summary: Deprecated `RecordStore` is still exported alongside the new store API via warning suppression
evidence: `crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs:21` exports `PostgresRecordStore` as a primary path, but `crates/temper-evolution/src/lib.rs:27-28` keeps exporting deprecated `RecordStore` behind `#[allow(deprecated)]`.
The crate root publicly exposes both legacy and current surfaces, so callers can continue binding to deprecated APIs indefinitely.
findings.suggestion: Remove `#[allow(deprecated)]` at crate root, stop re-exporting deprecated `RecordStore`, and provide a migration alias feature gate or explicit deprecation window in one release with a concrete removal version.

findings.summary: One-line facade modules look like migration shims with no expiration or ownership
evidence: `crates/temper-cli/src/util.rs` only re-exports `temper_spec::naming::{to_pascal_case, to_snake_case}`.
`crates/temper-mcp/src/repl.rs` only re-exports `temper_sandbox::repl::{ReplConfig, run_repl}`.
Both modules preserve alternate import paths without adding behavior, docs of migration status, or planned removal markers.
findings.suggestion: Either inline direct imports at call sites and delete these shim modules, or mark them as temporary compatibility layers with `#[deprecated(note = ...)]` and a tracked removal milestone.

findings.summary: Excessive narrative comments restate obvious code and add maintenance noise
evidence: In `crates/temper-evolution/src/records.rs`, most fields/enums are accompanied by obvious restating comments; this creates very high comment volume relative to logic.
`crates/temper-observe/src/schema.rs` includes large markdown-style column tables plus additional per-constant commentary for straightforward arrays and simple `contains` checks.

`crates/temper-optimize/src/types.rs` and `crates/temper-mcp/src/error.rs` similarly annotate nearly every variant/field with descriptive text that often repeats identifier meaning rather than documenting non-obvious constraints.
findings.suggestion: Trim comments to intent-bearing information (invariants, protocol constraints, edge cases), keep concise type-level docs, and remove per-field narration where names are already explicit.

findings.summary: Tool execution API exposes two competing error channels
evidence: `ToolRegistry::execute` returns `anyhow::Result<ToolResult>` (`tools/mod.rs:55`).
`ToolResult` includes `Error(String)` (`tools/mod.rs:27-32`), so failures may be represented as either `Err(_)` or `Ok(ToolResult::Error(_))`.
By contrast, MCP consolidates error propagation on `Result<T, McpError>` (`mcp/error.rs:61-63`), showing a clearer single-channel contract.
findings.suggestion: Collapse to one failure channel: either return `Result<String, ToolError>` (preferred) or return only `ToolResult` and remove outer `Result`. Introduce a typed `ToolError` enum and map it explicitly at protocol boundaries.

findings.summary: Authorization mapping API is infallible despite parsing dynamic input
evidence: `to_cedar(&self, name: &str, input: &Value) -> CedarMapping` is infallible (`tools/mod.rs:58`).
The function consumes untyped JSON and tool name strings, which can be malformed/unknown but has no error path.
Authz layer already defines validation-style failures (`InvalidPrincipal`, `InvalidResource`) (`authz/error.rs:14-24`), implying mapping/validation failures are first-class in this subsystem.
findings.suggestion: Change `to_cedar` to return `Result<CedarMapping, ToolAuthzMapError>` (or `AuthzError`) and enforce explicit handling for malformed tool payloads and unknown actions before policy evaluation.

findings.summary: OData parser functions are exported at multiple API depths
evidence: Crate root exports `parse_filter` and `parse_query_options` (`odata/lib.rs:19-22`).
The query module also exports the same functions (`odata/query/mod.rs:9-13`).
This creates two canonical import paths for the same operation and leads to inconsistent usage style across consumers.
findings.suggestion: Pick one canonical surface (either only crate-root convenience exports or only module-scoped exports) and deprecate the duplicate path with a documented migration window.

findings.summary: Evolution crate root exposes both current and deprecated store contracts
evidence: `temper-evolution` root exports `PostgresRecordStore` and also re-exports deprecated `RecordStore` with `#[allow(deprecated)]` (`evolution/lib.rs:21`, `evolution/lib.rs:27-28`).
Sibling crates like `temper-observe` expose a single current store interface (`ObservabilityStore`) at root (`observe/lib.rs:30-31`), highlighting API-shape drift.
Keeping both old and new store contracts in the primary surface makes the canonical integration path ambiguous for new consumers.
findings.suggestion: Move deprecated `RecordStore` behind a `legacy` module or feature flag, keep one root-level canonical store API, and publish a removal timeline in crate docs/changelog.
